backlog.title: Bug Fix Remediation — PRD Validation Findings
backlog.description: Address three issues identified during end-to-end PRD validation of the qmk-notifier multi-OS delta (session 002_c243e735980a). Issue 1 (Major): add a regression test locking in correct @-literal / \w-excludes-@ semantics for the PRD §11.2C gate. Issue 2 (Minor): fix sanitize_string to strip NUL bytes by explicit length instead of truncating at them. Issue 3 (Minor): add layer-track match/miss print to the CONSOLE_ENABLE debug block per PRD §8.6 step 9. No critical bugs; core matching/dispatch/reassembly functionality is verified correct and must remain untouched.

milestones.title: Issue 1 — Regression Test for @-Literal Matcher Semantics
milestones.description: The PRD §11.2C acceptance gate had a wrong expected value (user_host vs user@host). The PRD.md was already corrected at commit 4d49460. The matcher code (pattern_match.c) is correct and must NOT be modified. The remaining work is a regression test that locks in the correct semantics so a future 'rebuild to spec' cannot regress the matcher toward the broken expectation.

tasks.title: Add regression test cases for ^\w+@\w+$ literal-@ semantics
tasks.description: Add data-driven test cases to test_pattern_match.c that assert the correct behavior of the ^\w+@\w+$ pattern: it matches 'user@host' (literal @ present) and does NOT match 'user_host' (no @, underscore is \w). Both directions are essential — the user_host→false case is the one that prevents a future regression.

subtasks.title: Add @-literal regression test cases to test_pattern_match.c

milestones.title: Issue 2 — sanitize_string NUL Stripping Fix
milestones.description: sanitize_string (notifier.c:46-69) uses while (*read_ptr) which truncates at the first NUL byte instead of stripping it. The PRD spec (§8.2, §F2.3) says 'Strip every other byte' — NUL is 'every other byte' and should be stripped, preserving subsequent valid bytes. This is unreachable in normal operation (transport appends ETX before zero-fill) but is a theoretical robustness defect. Fix the function to iterate by explicit length and add a stub test verifying the fix through the hid_notify → process_full_message path.

tasks.title: Fix sanitize_string to strip NUL by iterating with explicit length
tasks.description: Change sanitize_string from a C-string loop (while *read_ptr) to an explicit-length loop so NUL bytes are encountered and stripped (skipped by the allowlist) rather than causing truncation. Update the single call site in hid_notify to pass msg_index as the length.

subtasks.title: Change sanitize_string signature to accept length and fix the iteration loop
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/002_c243e735980a/bugfix/001_4a8e381fbf10/architecture/findings_and_risks.md §Issue 2. sanitize_string is at notifier.c:46-69, declared `static void sanitize_string(char *str)`. It uses `while (*read_ptr)` (line 52) which stops at the first NUL byte. The allowlist at lines 55-61 correctly excludes 0x00 but the loop never reaches it. The function is `static` (file-local) with exactly ONE caller: hid_notify at notifier.c:495, where `msg_index` holds the count of valid payload bytes written to msg_buffer. PRD §8.2 spec says signature is `sanitize_string(char *str)` — this creates a documented spec drift (see findings_and_risks.md).
2. INPUT: No data from prior subtasks.
3. LOGIC:
   a) Change the signature at notifier.c:46 from:
        `static void sanitize_string(char *str)`
      to:
        `static void sanitize_string(char *str, size_t len)`
   b) Replace the `while (*read_ptr)` loop (line 52) with an index-bounded loop:
        `for (size_t i = 0; i < len; i++)`
      Read each byte via `read_ptr` (which still advances) or via `str[i]`. The allowlist condition (lines 55-61) stays unchanged. NUL bytes (0x00) are not in the allowlist → they are skipped (stripped), NOT truncating the scan.
   c) Keep the `*write_ptr = '\0'` NUL-termination at the end (line 67).
   d) Keep the NULL-input guard `if (!str) return;` (line 47). Add `if (len == 0) { *str = '\0'; return; }` or let the loop handle it (zero iterations → write_ptr == str → *str = '\0').
   e) Update the call site at notifier.c:495 from:
        `sanitize_string(msg_buffer);`
      to:
        `sanitize_string(msg_buffer, (size_t)msg_index);`
      This passes the count of bytes actually written to msg_buffer (before the NUL terminator was set). The NUL terminator `msg_buffer[msg_index] = '\0'` at line 494 should be REMOVED since sanitize_string now NUL-terminates internally (write_ptr ≤ str + len). If keeping it for safety, it is redundant but harmless.
   DO NOT modify the allowlist condition, the GS_DELIMITER/ETX_TERMINATOR checks, or any other function.
4. OUTPUT: notifier.c compiles clean with both the normal build and the stub-compiled build (run_notifier_stub_tests.sh). The existing 11 dispatch + 31 OS stub tests must still pass (0 FAIL lines). Verify: ./run_notifier_stub_tests.sh prints '✓ notifier stub-compile gate PASSED'.
5. DOCS: none — sanitize_string is static (not public API); the spec drift is documented in architecture/findings_and_risks.md. No user-facing/config surface change.

tasks.title: Add stub test for embedded-NUL sanitization through hid_notify
tasks.description: Add a test case to test_notifier_dispatch.c that sends a synthetic HID report containing an embedded NUL byte (0x00) in the payload, terminated by ETX. Verify that the message is correctly sanitized (NUL stripped, subsequent bytes preserved) rather than truncated at the NUL. This confirms the sanitize_string fix works end-to-end through the real hid_notify → process_full_message dispatch path.

subtasks.title: Add embedded-NUL sanitization test to test_notifier_dispatch.c
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/002_c243e735980a/bugfix/001_4a8e381fbf10/architecture/findings_and_risks.md §Issue 2 §Test approach. test_notifier_dispatch.c uses the ck() helper pattern: `static int g_pass=0, g_fail=0; static void ck(const char *p, const char *m, int cs, int want) {...}`. It re-declares external entry points from notifier.c (hid_notify, process_full_message, match_pattern). The test binary is compiled by run_notifier_stub_tests.sh which stub-compiles notifier.c and links it with qmk_stubs.c. The existing summary format is 'Total tests run: N / passed: N / failed: N' and FAIL: lines are counted by the harness.
2. INPUT: The fixed sanitize_string from P1.M2.T1.S1 (now accepts size_t len and strips NUL). The hid_notify entry point is accessible (external linkage). A command map with DEFINE_SERIAL_COMMANDS is available in the test file.
3. LOGIC: Add a new test scenario block in main() that:
   a) Defines a command map entry with a pattern that matches the post-NUL portion of the message — e.g. pattern "*world*" (case-insensitive) — so that if the NUL is stripped (correct), the pattern matches and the callback fires.
   b) Constructs a raw HID report array: {0x81, 0x9F, 'h','e','l','l','o', 0x00, 'w','o','r','l','d', 0x03} (magic header + payload with embedded NUL + ETX terminator). The 0x00 is at index 7 (after 'hello', before 'world').
   c) Calls hid_notify(report, sizeof_report).
   d) Asserts that the callback fired (matching the pattern), proving the NUL was stripped and 'world' was preserved in the dispatched message.
   e) ALSO add a negative-control assertion: verify that a DIFFERENT pattern that would match only the pre-NUL prefix (e.g. "hello") does NOT match alone — confirming the full sanitized message 'helloworld' (not truncated 'hello') was dispatched. Or alternatively, use a pattern like "hello*world" that requires both portions.
   Use the existing ck() helper or CK() macro pattern. Increment g_pass/g_fail.
4. OUTPUT: test_notifier_dispatch.c compiles and passes with 0 FAIL lines. run_notifier_stub_tests.sh prints '✓ notifier stub-compile gate PASSED'. The new test count appears in the summary.
5. DOCS: none — test-only change, no user-facing surface.

milestones.title: Issue 3 — CONSOLE_ENABLE Layer Track Debug Print + Changeset-Level Docs Sync
milestones.description: The #ifdef CONSOLE_ENABLE block at notifier.c:417-434 prints only the command-track match/miss. The PRD §8.6 step 9 says 'print per-track match/miss.' Add a layer-track uprintf mirroring the command track. Then sync changeset-level documentation (README.md test counts, overview) across all three issues.

tasks.title: Add layer-track match/miss print to CONSOLE_ENABLE debug block
tasks.description: Add a layer-track uprintf to the #ifdef CONSOLE_ENABLE block in process_full_message, after the existing command-track print. Create a minimal qmk_stubs/print.h stub so the code can be compile-checked with -DCONSOLE_ENABLE.

subtasks.title: Add layer-track uprintf and create print.h stub for CONSOLE_ENABLE compile-check
subtasks.context_scope:
CONTRACT DEFINITION:
1. RESEARCH NOTE: See plan/002_c243e735980a/bugfix/001_4a8e381fbf10/architecture/findings_and_risks.md §Issue 3. The CONSOLE_ENABLE block is at notifier.c:417-434 inside process_full_message. It currently prints only the command track (lines 429-433): match → 'Matched message %s on command: %s' using command_found->pattern; miss → 'Did not match message %s on any command'. The GS→'|' substitution (lines 418-423) already runs on the local received_command buffer before printing. Variables in scope: received_command[256] (local buffer, GS already replaced with '|'), command_found (command_map_t*, NULL if miss), layer_found (uint8_t, LAYER_UNSET=255 if miss). uprintf is declared via #include "print.h" (notifier.c:20-21, guarded by #ifdef CONSOLE_ENABLE). The stub harness does NOT provide print.h or uprintf.
2. INPUT: No data from prior subtasks (Issue 3 is independent of Issues 1 and 2). However, if P1.M2.T1.S1 has been applied, the CONSOLE_ENABLE block line numbers may shift by ~2 lines (sanitize_string grows by adding a parameter). Work from the current file state.
3. LOGIC:
   a) In the #ifdef CONSOLE_ENABLE block of process_full_message, AFTER the existing command-track if/else (currently ending at line 433), add a layer-track print:
        if (layer_found != LAYER_UNSET) {
            uprintf("Matched message %s on layer: %d\n", received_command, layer_found);
        } else {
            uprintf("Did not match message %s on any layer\n", received_command);
        }
      This mirrors the command-track output format. The layer index is sufficient for debugging (storing a layer_map_t* for the pattern would require dispatch-logic changes not warranted for a Minor debug issue). Both prints are inside the existing #ifdef CONSOLE_ENABLE guard and use the already GS→'|' substituted received_command buffer.
   b) Create qmk_stubs/print.h — a minimal stub for host compile-checks:
        #pragma once
        #include <stdio.h>
        #define uprintf(...) printf(__VA_ARGS__)
      This file is ONLY included when building with -DCONSOLE_ENABLE; the stub harness normally builds without it.
   c) Compile-check: run a one-off gcc build with CONSOLE_ENABLE to verify no errors:
        gcc -Wall -Wextra -std=c99 -DCONSOLE_ENABLE \
            -DQMK_KEYBOARD_H='"qmk_keyboard_stub.h"' \
            -Iqmk_stubs -I. -c notifier.c -o /tmp/notifier_console_check.o
      Verify: clean compilation, no new warnings.
   DO NOT change the command-track print, the GS→'|' substitution loop, or any dispatch logic.
4. OUTPUT: notifier.c compiles clean both WITHOUT CONSOLE_ENABLE (normal build, run_notifier_stub_tests.sh passes) AND WITH CONSOLE_ENABLE (the one-off compile check above). No behavior change in production (CONSOLE_ENABLE is off in all automated test gates).
5. DOCS: none — CONSOLE_ENABLE debug output is not a user-facing/config surface change. The print.h stub is test infrastructure, not user-facing.

tasks.title: Sync changeset-level documentation
tasks.description: Final documentation sweep across all three bug fixes. Update README.md test count table to reflect new test cases added to test_pattern_match.c (Issue 1) and test_notifier_dispatch.c (Issue 2). Review and update any overview/feature docs that reference test counts, sanitize_string behavior, or CONSOLE_ENABLE debug output.
